title: Hardware Security Requirements
description: Advanced hardware-level security requirements for heterogeneous board architectures.

items.description: Enforce full signature verification at all boot stages using custom keys embedded in UEFI (x86) and MB1/MB2 (NVIDIA).
Leverage hardware eFuses or secure counters to physically block the execution of downgraded, vulnerable firmware versions.
Generate and store all private cryptographic keys strictly within a TEE (OP-TEE) or TPM 2.0, preventing extraction to the normal OS environment.
Utilize unique, physical chip characteristics (PUF) to generate immutable device IDs, preventing hardware cloning and issuing tied certificates.
Physically or logically disable hardware debugging ports (via fuse blowing) in all mass-production and partner-distributed units.
Enable the IOMMU to enforce hardware-level isolation, preventing device drivers from accessing unauthorized memory regions (e.g., between AI models and control logic).
Equip the robot chassis with light sensors or mechanical switches. Upon unauthorized opening, automatically trigger the destruction (Self-Destruction) of cryptographic keys inside the TEE.
Implement independent watchdog circuits monitoring the ethernet link between NVIDIA and x86 boards at a 1ms interval. Engage hardware brakes immediately upon physical disconnection.

title: System Software Security Requirements
description: Runtime system integrity, OS-level hardening, and data protection.

items.description: All security operations must be based on a unique identifier and keys anchored in hardware (eFuse, TPM 2.0, or TEE).
A verifiable chain must be established where each stage (ROM → Bootloader → Kernel → RootFS) validates the digital signature of the next.
Hardware must be fused with unique signing keys generated by the developer, rather than relying on default OEM keys (NVIDIA/Intel).
Hardware security counters must be implemented to prevent the downgrade of firmware to older, vulnerable versions.
For production and partner-distributed units, physical debugging interfaces (JTAG, UART, etc.) must be hardware-disabled (via Fuse Blowing).
System partitions (/bin, /usr, etc.) must be mounted as Read-Only. Any unauthorized write attempts must trigger a hardware exception.
The kernel must be compiled with modern security features, including KASLR, Stack Protector, and Control Flow Integrity (CFI).
Core control logic and AI training processes must run under strictly separated privileges (User/Group) to prevent cross-interference.
cgroups-based resource allocation must be applied to prevent any single process from monopolizing CPU/Memory and causing a DoS.
All unnecessary network services, daemons, and shell tools (e.g., compilers) must be removed to minimize the attack surface.
All non-volatile storage (SSD, eMMC) must be encrypted using AES-256 or higher algorithms.
Decryption keys must be stored within the TPM/TEE and released only if the Secure Boot process is verified as successful.
Core binaries must undergo stripping and obfuscation. Execution should ideally occur in a structure where decryption happens only within the TEE.
All security keys (SSH, TLS, API Keys) must never be stored in plaintext within the filesystem and must be managed within the hardware secure world.
Mechanical switches or light sensors must be installed to detect chassis opening and trigger an immediate security event.
In the event of a severe physical breach, the system must support "zeroization"—the self-destruction of core encryption keys stored in the TEE.
External ports (USB, LAN) must implement a kernel-level whitelist policy to disable data communication with unauthorized devices.
Enable Kernel Address Space Layout Randomization (KASLR) to prevent attackers from predicting the memory addresses of critical kernel functions.
Enable Privileged Execute Never (PXN) and Privileged Access Never (PAN) to block the kernel from executing malicious code or accessing data in user-space memory.
Compile all kernel modules and core binaries with 'Stack Canaries' to detect and neutralize Buffer Overflow attacks in real-time.
Set non-volatile kernel data structures to Read-Only mode to prevent runtime tampering of the kernel’s internal state.
Implement strict AppArmor (Linux Security Module) profiles for ROS 2 nodes and sensor drivers, restricting access only to pre-authorized files and network sockets.
All robotic applications must run under dedicated non-root system accounts. The use of sudo must be strictly audited and restricted to authorized administrative tasks.
Instead of granting full Root access, assign only the minimum required Linux Capabilities (e.g., CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_RAWIO) to specific processes.
Optimize /etc/sysctl.conf to disable IP Forwarding, ignore ICMP Redirects, and enable SYN Flood protection to harden the network stack.
Configure modprobe to blacklist usb-storage and uas modules, preventing the introduction of malware via physical USB ports.
If remote access is required, disable password authentication in favor of SSH Key-based authentication, prohibit Root login, and use non-standard ports.
Deploy the Integrity Measurement Architecture (IMA) to verify that executable files and configuration files have not been tampered with during runtime.
Ensure all software updates originate only from officially signed repositories. Prohibit the installation of unsigned third-party packages.

title: Audit Logging & Traceability Requirements
description: System and security event logging, monitoring, and remote log forwarding.

items.description: Utilize the Linux Audit Framework (auditd) to record security-sensitive system calls in real-time, including execve (process execution), ptrace (debugging), and setuid (privilege escalation).
Log all sudo usage history, SSH login attempts, and any access or modification attempts to critical configuration files such as /etc/shadow and /etc/sudoers.
Record events where CPU/Memory usage exceeds predefined thresholds or when a process monopolizes resources, to detect signs of Denial of Service (DoS) attacks.
Configure local log files as 'Append-Only' (using chattr +a) to prevent the modification or deletion of existing records.
Hash each log block using a key stored within the Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) and link them in a chain to immediately detect if logs are missing or reordered.
To prevent evidence destruction on the local board, forward logs immediately to a cloud SIEM or a remote secure server via an encrypted mTLS channel.
Maintain a high-precision timestamped correlation between the Command ID received from the Cloud VLM and the physical motor actuation performed by the robot.
Store signature metadata alongside logs to prove that vision/audio data sent to the cloud originated from authentic physical sensor hardware.
Record every instance of a hardware E-Stop trigger or software Fail-Safe activation, accompanied by a system state snapshot at the time of the event.
Monitor for abnormal behaviors such as SSH access during off-hours, repeated authentication failures, or massive data transfers to unauthorized IPs.
For critical violations (e.g., chassis intrusion per SYS-15, key zeroization per SYS-16), trigger an immediate administrative alert within 5 seconds.
Upon suspicion of a breach, automatically dump volatile memory states and active process lists to secure storage for post-incident investigation.

title: Cloud-Robot Communication Security
description: Security for data exchange between robot and cloud VLM.

items.description: Establish a bidirectional trust relationship using TLS 1.3, where both the robot and the cloud server must verify each other's certificates.
The private keys used for mTLS must be stored in a hardware security module (TPM 2.0 or TEE) and never be exportable to the OS.
All vision/audio streams and control commands must be encrypted from the point of origin to the final destination, preventing plaintext exposure at intermediate nodes.
Use cipher suites that support Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) to ensure that past communications remain secure even if the long-term server key is compromised.
Every high-level instruction from the cloud must be digitally signed by a Hardware Security Module (HSM) on the server-side, and verified by the robot's secure world.
All control packets must include a unique Nonce and a high-precision Timestamp to block attacks that retransmit previously captured legitimate commands.
Implement a strictly tiered Role-Based Access Control system to separate "Observation/Telemetry" privileges from "Physical Actuation" (Motor Control).
Control commands must have a minimal Time-to-Live (e.g., <100ms). Expired commands must be discarded immediately to prevent delayed execution hazards.
Establish a dedicated secure tunnel (e.g., WireGuard or IPsec VPN) between the robot's local network and the cloud VPC for all management traffic.
Implement the QUIC protocol to minimize head-of-line blocking and reduce handshake latency, ensuring smooth high-definition video transmission for VLM analysis.
Enforce strict rate limits on inbound commands to mitigate Command Flooding or Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks originating from the cloud side.
In the event of a communication timeout (Heartbeat loss), the robot must autonomously trigger a "Safe Stop" or "Local Autonomy" mode within a predefined safety window.
Implement edge-side masking of sensitive information (e.g., human faces) before transmitting visual data to the cloud to comply with privacy regulations.
Vision frames must be tagged with a digital watermark or metadata-hash signed within the TEE to guarantee that the data originated from the authentic sensor hardware.

title: Local Network Security
description: Inter-board communication and internal network isolation.

items.description: All communications between the x86 and NVIDIA boards must implement mutual TLS (mTLS) based on TLS 1.3 or HMAC-based message authentication to verify the source of every packet.
Utilize VLAN (Virtual LAN) configurations to strictly isolate high-bandwidth vision data (Data Plane) from low-bandwidth, mission-critical control signals (Control Plane).
To prevent IP Spoofing within the internal network, static IP addresses must be assigned, and MAC addresses must be statically bound to the ARP table to block unauthorized device intervention.
Enable SROS2 (DDS-Security) to enforce encryption, authentication, and access control policies for all internal ROS 2 nodes and communication topics.
Establish a mutual heartbeat exchange with a cycle of 10ms or less. Upon detection of communication loss or latency exceeding thresholds, both boards must immediately trigger a "Safe Stop" state.

The NVIDIA board shall periodically verify the integrity of the x86 board (e.g., Audit log summaries, kernel signatures). Upon detecting anomalies, it must alert the cloud and revoke control authority.
Implement bandwidth constraints on inter-board traffic below physical limits to prevent a "Traffic Flood" (DoS) from an infected node from paralyzing the entire control system.
Disable all unused physical and logical ports on internal switches and boards. Implement a whitelist-based access policy for authorized devices on any exposed ports.
Deploy a lightweight Intrusion Detection System (IDS) to monitor internal Ethernet traffic for abnormal protocol calls or suspicious high-volume data requests.

Session keys for inter-board encryption must never be stored on disk. They must be dynamically generated and exchanged via a secure channel between the Trusted Execution Environments (TEE) of each board.

title: Application Security Requirements
description: Cloud AI (VLM) communication and remote control integrity.

items.description: Generate a digital signature/watermark inside the TEE immediately upon sensor data creation (e.g., video frames) to prevent manipulation in transit to the VLM.
Embed a digital signature generated by the server-side HSM into every cloud-issued control command. The robot must verify this signature locally before execution.
Prevent compromised robots from receiving commands by enforcing 'Remote Attestation'. Verify the robot's integrity state before issuing commands, blocking access upon violation.
Enforce a strict commands-per-second rate limit to defend against command flooding if a cloud account is compromised. Terminate sessions instantly upon detecting anomalous patterns.
Implement a local 'Rule-based Checker' to validate outputs from the cloud VLM. Physically impossible or hazardous commands must be intercepted and blocked prior to execution.
Enhance privacy by extracting only necessary feature vectors or performing local de-identification (e.g., face masking) at the application layer before cloud transmission.
Ensure anonymized video/audio data is stored only within compliant, designated cloud regions. Enforce automated data destruction policies upon expiration of the retention period.
